transcript.content:
**Honestly, I want both and can't rank them.**

And I don't think that's a cop-out — I think in this work, they're genuinely inseparable. If you've built something meaningful but no one in the field knows it happened, you lose the ability to replicate it, to bring others along, to attract the resources that sustain it. I've watched incredible programs quietly disappear because the people running them didn't cultivate the relationships that would have protected them. So when I think about five years from now, I want to look back at real impact — donor relationships that turned into lasting partnerships, staff I mentored who are now leading their own teams — and yes, I want the people who can amplify that work to know my name. That's not ego. That's strategy in service of the mission.

When you imagine a version of yourself who completely stopped tracking whether the right people knew your name — stopped cultivating that visibility entirely — would that feel like freedom, or would something important go missing?

**Showed up for someone — the relationship is where it lives.**

Though I'd push back a little on the framing, because for me those two things are almost inseparable — the execution *is* the showing up. Like, last spring we had a donor who was ready to walk away from a capital campaign pledge after a family health crisis, and the reason he stayed wasn't the proposal I rewrote at midnight, it was that I'd remembered his daughter's name from eighteen months earlier and asked about her first. The impact happened because the relationship was already there. I think people who are really good at this work understand that the "result" is almost always downstream of the connection.
